<findings>

[observation] [warning] 23 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports

</findings>

<insights>

- 23 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
- At 47 files and 23 entry points this is the largest non-analyze zone; it risks becoming a catch-all — periodically audit whether subsets (e.g., sync-engine + sync) have grown cohesive enough to extract.
- The balanced cohesion/coupling (0.51/0.49) is healthy for a domain core; it absorbs stable internal dependencies without over-exporting to peers.
- Heavy inbound imports from unit-analyze (67) and cli (38) confirm this zone is the true domain foundation — keeping it import-free from those layers is architecturally correct.
- 23 declared entry points is unusually high; consider auditing which are truly public API versus internal cross-file imports that leaked into the entry-point list.
- sync-engine.ts and sync.ts alongside canonical.ts suggest sync-related logic is partially here and partially in the store zone — verify ownership is intentional and not an accidental split.
- public.ts is an entry point in this zone, correctly placing the package's public API surface at the domain core layer.
- The 2 imports from prd-core-operations INTO task-selection (core→task: 2 crossings) are architecturally suspicious — core domain logic should not depend on a feature-level selection module; these 2 imports likely represent a shared concept (e.g. task scoring type) that belongs lower in the hierarchy.
- prd-core-operations imports from task-selection (2 crossings) — a domain core importing from a feature module inverts the expected dependency direction; extract the shared concept into prd-core-operations or a shared type module.
- The 23 declared entry points span types, utilities, analytics, sync, health, and keyword logic — a breadth suggesting this zone functions as the package's default dumping ground for any logic that doesn't obviously belong elsewhere. The absence of a forcing function (no sub-package boundary, no explicit access control) means the zone will continue to accumulate files as features are added.
- prd-core-operations has 23 entry points across fundamentally different concerns (analytics, sync, health, keywords, code-coverage, DAG). Unlike a well-defined public API, this breadth means any change to the zone's internals potentially breaks consumers across all 23 surfaces. A versioned public.ts facade that re-exports only intentionally public symbols would contain blast radius.
- analytics.ts and code-coverage.ts both read from the .hench/ run history directory — they depend on a directory produced by a sibling package (hench) through filesystem path convention rather than through a gateway import. This creates a hidden cross-package dependency that bypasses the gateway pattern documented in CLAUDE.md and will silently break if hench changes its run directory structure or output format.
- sync-engine.ts, sync.ts, and canonical.ts together form a nascent sync subsystem within prd-core-operations that shares a clear data flow (canonical serialization → sync engine → sync command) distinct from the analytics, health, keywords, and DAG subgroups also in this zone. All three files couple primarily to prd-storage-integrations. Extracting them into a dedicated prd-sync zone is the lowest-risk reduction available because no new cross-zone edges would be created — the coupling is already targeted.
- analytics.ts and code-coverage.ts read from .hench/ run history via filesystem path rather than a gateway import — this is an undocumented cross-package dependency on hench's directory layout. Add a typed path constant or minimal gateway to formalize this contract before hench restructures its output.
- Extract sync-engine.ts + sync.ts + canonical.ts into a dedicated prd-sync zone. These three files share a serialization→engine→command data flow and couple primarily to prd-storage-integrations, making this the cleanest available split from the 47-file mega-zone with no new coupling introduced.
- [call graph] 1591 internal calls, 77 outgoing, 128 incoming (cohesion: 0.95, coupling: 0.05)

</insights>
